# Pyxle

> Python and React in one file. A Python-first full-stack framework where @server loaders and @action mutations sit beside real React 18 (SSR + hydration) in a single .pyxl file — file-based routing, no API plumbing, no separate front-end build to wire up.

## Getting Started

- [Introduction](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/introduction): Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework that brings the Next.js developer experience to the Python ecosystem. You write your server logic in Python, your UI in React, and ship them together i
- [Installation](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/installation): Pyxle requires:
- [Quick Start](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/quick-start): Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes.
- [Project Structure](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/project-structure): After running `pyxle init my-app`, you get this file tree:

## Core Concepts

- [.pyxl Files](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/pyxl-files): A `.pyxl` file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file -- your data fetching and your UI live together.
- [Routing](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/routing): Pyxle uses **file-based routing**. The file structure inside your `pages/` directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed.
- [Data Loading](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/data-loading): Pyxle uses `@server` decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the Re
- [Server Actions](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/server-actions): Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to `@server` loaders -- loaders read data, actions write data.
- [Layouts](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/layouts): Layouts wrap pages in shared UI -- navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations.

## Guides

- [Pyxle vs. other frameworks](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/comparison): Pyxle isn't the only way to build a full-stack app in Python, and it isn't the right tool for every job. This page is an honest map of the landscape — what each alternative is genuinely great at, and
- [Migrating from Flask or Django](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/migration-from-flask-django): This guide maps the concepts you already know from **Flask** and **Django** to their Pyxle equivalents, so you can port an app a route at a time. It assumes you've read [Core Concepts](../core-concept
- [Styling](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/styling): Pyxle ships with Tailwind CSS configured in a fresh `pyxle init` project. The recommended way to load your stylesheets is to **import them from a JSX module** so Vite can compile, bundle, and content-
- [Head Management](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/head-management): Pyxle offers two ways to control the document `<head>`: the `<Head>` component (**recommended**) and the `HEAD` Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — bu
- [Caching](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching): By default a page's `@server` loader runs on **every request** and the page is rendered fresh each time. For pages whose content is the same for everyone and changes rarely — a marketing page, a docs
- [Build Optimization](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/build-optimization): `pyxle build` produces a hashed, minified, code-split client bundle and a matching SSR shell. Most of the optimization is automatic; this guide covers what you get for free, how to inspect it, and how
- [Streaming SSR](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming): By default Pyxle renders a page to its complete HTML string and then sends it. For a page with a slow part — a section that waits on a third-party API, a heavy below-the-fold widget — the visitor star
- [WebSockets](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/websockets): A Pyxle page can serve a **WebSocket** alongside its HTML. Add a module-level `async def websocket(ws)` to a page's Python section and Pyxle registers a WebSocket route at the page's path — the same f
- [API Routes](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/api-routes): Files under `pages/api/` are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not `.pyxl`) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses.
- [Background Tasks](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/background-tasks): Some work shouldn't make the user wait: sending a welcome email, emitting a webhook, warming a cache. Pyxle gives you two ways to run it off the request's critical path — pick by *when* the work shoul
- [Middleware](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware): Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: **application-level middleware** that wraps every request, and **route-level hooks** that run only for specific route types.
- [Plugins](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/plugins): Pyxle plugins are reusable pieces of functionality — database layers, auth systems, storage adapters — that a host app composes via `pyxle.config.json` rather than hand-wiring in its entry point. The
- [Environment Variables](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/environment-variables): Pyxle loads environment variables from `.env` files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and client-side injection.
- [Error Handling](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/error-handling): Pyxle provides structured error handling through error exceptions, error boundaries, and not-found pages.
- [Client Components](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/client-components): Pyxle provides built-in React components and hooks importable from `pyxle/client`.
- [Security](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security): Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box.
- [Observability](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability): Pyxle gives every request a **correlation id** and measures how long it takes — on by default, with no setup. Both are cheap enough (a `uuid4` and two `perf_counter` reads, no I/O) to run on every req
- [Deployment](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/deployment): Deploy a Pyxle application with `pyxle build` to compile assets and `pyxle serve` to run in production.
- [Editor setup](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/editor-setup): Pyxle Language Tools brings first-class IDE support to `.pyxl` files — syntax highlighting, diagnostics, completions, hover documentation, go-to-definition, and formatting. Everything works out of the
- [TypeScript](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/typescript): Pyxle gives you typed editor support and an opt-in type-check gate **without** asking you to write TypeScript in your `.pyxl` files. The model is **consumption, not authoring**: your server code is ty
- [Pyxle for AI coding agents](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/for-ai-agents): Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real full-stack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per
- [Migrating from .pyx to .pyxl](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/migration-pyx-to-pyxl): Pyxle's file extension has changed from `.pyx` to `.pyxl`. This guide explains why and how to update your project.

## Reference

- [CLI Reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/cli): The `pyxle` CLI manages Pyxle projects from scaffolding to production.
- [Configuration Reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration): Pyxle is configured via `pyxle.config.json` in the project root. All fields are optional -- sensible defaults are used when omitted.
- [Runtime API Reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/runtime-api): The `pyxle.runtime` module provides decorators and error classes used in `.pyxl` files. The compiler **auto-injects** a small set: the `@server` decorator, and — in any file that declares at least one
- [Client API Reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/client-api): All client-side components and hooks are importable from `pyxle/client`:
- [Plugins API Reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/plugins-api): Every public symbol in `pyxle.plugins`, including the access helpers introduced alongside the plugin system.

## Plugins

- [pyxle-db](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-db): `pyxle-db` is Pyxle's official database plugin over **SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL**. It offers two first-class paths: an **explicit-SQL** API (portable placeholders, a uniform `Row` type, checksum-t
- [pyxle-auth](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-auth): `pyxle-auth` is Pyxle's official authentication plugin: email + password accounts, sliding sessions, password-reset and email-verification flows, role-based access control, and scoped API tokens — wir
- [pyxle-mail](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-mail): `pyxle-mail` is Pyxle's official email plugin: one `mail.service` your loaders, actions, and API routes call as `await mail.send(...)`, delivered over SMTP, [Resend](https://resend.com), or any backen
- [Plugin Standards](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/standards): The bar a plugin must clear to be listed in the [pyxle.dev plugin directory](/plugins). It exists so that anything a Pyxle user installs from the directory behaves the way the official plugins do — pr
- [Plugin Ideas](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/ideas): Capabilities the ecosystem needs, scoped to what the plugin API supports **today** — services and middleware (see [the plugins guide](../guides/plugins.md); page contribution is [Phase B, in RFC](rfc-
- [RFC: Plugin Routes & Pages (Phase B)](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/rfc-plugin-pages): Phase A (shipped, stable) lets a plugin run lifecycle hooks, register named services, and contribute ASGI **middleware** — see the [plugins guide](../guides/plugins.md). What it cannot do is own a URL

## Architecture

- [Pyxle Architecture](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/README): Welcome. This section is the **architecture handbook** — a guided tour of how Pyxle is built on the inside, written for everyone from "I just installed Pyxle yesterday" to "I want to send a PR that to
- [Overview — A request, end to end](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/overview): This is the one doc to read first. We're going to follow a single HTTP request all the way from `localhost:8000/` to your browser, naming every component it touches and every transformation it goes th
- [The .pyxl file format](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/pyxl-files): A Pyxle page is a single file with two languages in it: Python and JSX. This doc explains *why* that file exists, *what's actually in it*, and
- [The parser](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/parser): The honest answer is: by **parsing**. The Pyxle parser feeds the source to Python's `ast.parse` and uses the structure of the result — and the structure of the failure when parsing fails — to find the
- [The compiler](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/compiler): The compiler is the bridge between the parser and the rest of the framework. The parser produces an in-memory `PyxParseResult`; the compiler turns that result into **three files on disk** that the dev
- [Routing](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/routing): Pyxle uses **file-based routing**: the path of a `.pyxl` file inside your `pages/` directory *is* its URL. There is no `@app.route("/")` decorator. There is no router config file. There is no list of
- [The dev server](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/dev-server): `pyxle dev` is the command you'll spend the most time with. It runs a Starlette ASGI app on port 8000, a Vite subprocess on port 5173, an SSR worker pool, an incremental compiler, a file watcher, and
- [Server-side rendering](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/ssr): Server-side rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a `PageRoute` and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robus
- [Build and serve](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/build-and-serve): `pyxle dev` is what you run during development. `pyxle build` and `pyxle serve` are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes:
- [The runtime](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/runtime): The "runtime" of Pyxle is the **smallest module in the framework**. It contains two decorators (`@server` and `@action`), two exception classes (`LoaderError` and `ActionError`), and one small helper
- [The CLI](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/cli): `pyxle` is the command-line entry point users actually type. It's a

## Advanced

- [SSR Pipeline](https://pyxle.dev/docs/advanced/ssr-pipeline): This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance.
- [Compiler Internals](https://pyxle.dev/docs/advanced/compiler-internals): The Pyxle compiler transforms `.pyxl` files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works.

## FAQ

- [Frequently Asked Questions](https://pyxle.dev/docs/faq): Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in `.pyxl` files, providing file-based routing, server-side rendering, and a development experie

## Changelog

- [Changelog](https://pyxle.dev/docs/changelog): Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (`0.x`), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run `pip install --upgrade pyxle-framework`.

## More

- [Plugin directory](https://pyxle.dev/plugins): official and community plugins
- [Benchmarks & methodology](https://pyxle.dev/benchmarks): reproducible performance numbers
- [Interactive playground](https://pyxle.dev/playground): run Pyxle in the browser
